notes.heading: Introduction to Ray Optics
notes.text: Ray optics, also called geometrical optics, is the branch of optics in which light is treated as travelling in straight lines called rays. It studies how light travels, reflects, refracts, forms images, and works in optical instruments like microscopes and telescopes. Ray optics is useful when the size of the object or obstacle is much larger than the wavelength of light.
points: A ray of light is an imaginary straight line showing the path of light.
Light travels in a straight line in a uniform medium.
Visible light has wavelength approximately from 4 × 10^-7 m to 7 × 10^-7 m.
Ray optics is used for mirrors, lenses, prisms, microscopes, and telescopes.
Basic principles of ray optics are rectilinear propagation, reflection, and refraction.
Reflection is the return of light into the same medium after striking a surface.
Refraction is the bending of light when it passes from one medium to another.
Incident ray is the ray falling on a surface.
Reflected ray is the ray that returns after reflection.
Refracted ray is the ray that bends and enters another medium.
Normal is the perpendicular drawn to the surface at the point of incidence.
Ray Optics = Study of light using light rays.

notes.heading: Reflection of Light and Laws of Reflection
notes.text: Reflection of light is the phenomenon in which light falls on a smooth polished surface and comes back into the same medium. A plane mirror is a common example of reflection.
points: Incident ray is the ray that strikes the reflecting surface.
Reflected ray is the ray that comes back after reflection.
Point of incidence is the point where the incident ray strikes the surface.
Normal is a perpendicular line drawn to the reflecting surface at the point of incidence.
Angle of incidence i is the angle between the incident ray and the normal.
Angle of reflection r is the angle between the reflected ray and the normal.
First law of reflection: The incident ray, reflected ray, and normal all lie in the same plane.
Second law of reflection: Angle of incidence is equal to angle of reflection.
Formula: i = r.
If angle of incidence is 30°, angle of reflection is also 30°.
A plane mirror is a flat reflecting surface.
Image formed by a plane mirror is virtual, erect, same size as object, laterally inverted, and formed as far behind the mirror as the object is in front.
For a plane mirror: Object distance = Image distance.
Lateral inversion means sideways reversal, such as left appearing as right in a mirror.
Reflection explains image formation by plane mirrors, concave mirrors, convex mirrors, and reflecting telescopes.

notes.heading: Spherical Mirrors
notes.text: A spherical mirror is a mirror whose reflecting surface is a part of a hollow sphere. Spherical mirrors are of two types: concave mirror and convex mirror.
points: A concave mirror has its reflecting surface curved inward and is called a converging mirror.
A concave mirror can form real and inverted images as well as virtual and erect images depending on object position.
A convex mirror has its reflecting surface curved outward and is called a diverging mirror.
A convex mirror always forms a virtual, erect, and diminished image.
Pole P is the centre point of the reflecting surface.
Centre of curvature C is the centre of the sphere of which the mirror is a part.
Radius of curvature R is the distance between pole and centre of curvature: R = PC.
Principal axis is the straight line passing through pole and centre of curvature.
Principal focus F is the point where rays parallel to the principal axis meet after reflection or appear to come from after reflection.
Focal length f is the distance between pole and focus: f = PF.
Relation between radius of curvature and focal length: R = 2f or f = R/2.
For a concave mirror, parallel rays after reflection pass through the focus.
For a concave mirror, rays passing through the focus become parallel after reflection.
For a concave mirror, rays passing through the centre of curvature return along the same path.
For a convex mirror, parallel rays after reflection appear to come from the focus behind the mirror.
For a convex mirror, rays directed towards the focus become parallel after reflection.
For a convex mirror, rays directed towards the centre of curvature return along the same path.
Uses of concave mirror: torches, searchlights, vehicle headlights, shaving mirrors, dental mirrors, and solar cookers.
Uses of convex mirror: rear-view mirrors in vehicles, security mirrors in shops, and road safety mirrors at blind turns.
Convex mirrors are used where a wide field of view is needed.

notes.heading: Sign Convention for Spherical Mirrors
notes.text: The New Cartesian Sign Convention is used to decide whether distances in mirror problems are positive or negative. It helps solve numerical problems consistently.
points: All distances are measured from the pole P of the mirror.
The principal axis is taken as the x-axis.
Distances measured in the direction of incident light are positive.
Distances measured opposite to the direction of incident light are negative.
Object distance u is usually negative because the object is placed in front of the mirror.
For a concave mirror, focal length f is negative because focus lies in front of the mirror.
For a convex mirror, focal length f is positive because focus lies behind the mirror.
Real image distance v is negative because real image is formed in front of the mirror.
Virtual image distance v is positive because virtual image is formed behind the mirror.
Object height ho is usually positive because object is above the principal axis.
Erect image height hi is positive.
Inverted image height hi is negative.
Concave mirror: u negative, f negative, R negative, real image v negative, virtual image v positive.
Convex mirror: u negative, f positive, R positive, virtual image v positive.
Important memory point: Concave mirror has negative focal length and convex mirror has positive focal length.

notes.heading: Mirror Formula and Magnification
notes.text: The mirror formula relates focal length, image distance, and object distance for spherical mirrors. Magnification tells how large or small the image is compared to the object.
points: Mirror formula: 1/f = 1/v + 1/u.
f is focal length, v is image distance, and u is object distance.
All values must be used with proper sign convention.
Object distance u is usually negative for mirror problems.
For real images, v is negative; for virtual images, v is positive.
For concave mirrors, f is negative; for convex mirrors, f is positive.
Magnification formula: m = hi/ho.
For mirrors: m = hi/ho = -v/u.
If m is positive, image is virtual and erect.
If m is negative, image is real and inverted.
If |m| > 1, image is enlarged.
If |m| < 1, image is diminished.
If |m| = 1, image is same size as object.
Example: For a concave mirror with f = -10 cm and u = -30 cm, using 1/f = 1/v + 1/u gives v = -15 cm.
For the same example, m = -v/u = -(-15)/(-30) = -1/2.
The image in the example is real, inverted, and half the size of the object.

notes.heading: Image Formation by Concave Mirror
notes.text: A concave mirror is a converging mirror. It forms different types of images depending on the position of the object.
points: A concave mirror can form real and inverted, virtual and erect, enlarged, diminished, or same-size images.
Important points on the principal axis are P (pole), F (focus), and C (centre of curvature).
C = 2F, so R = 2f.
Ray rule 1: A ray parallel to the principal axis passes through the focus after reflection.
Ray rule 2: A ray passing through the focus becomes parallel to the principal axis after reflection.
Ray rule 3: A ray passing through the centre of curvature returns along the same path.
Object at infinity: image forms at F, real, inverted, and highly diminished.
Object beyond C: image forms between C and F, real, inverted, and diminished.
Object at C: image forms at C, real, inverted, and same size; m = -1.
Object between C and F: image forms beyond C, real, inverted, and enlarged.
Object at F: image forms at infinity, real, inverted, and highly enlarged.
Object between F and P: image forms behind the mirror, virtual, erect, and enlarged.
When the object moves from infinity towards the mirror, the image moves from focus to infinity and then behind the mirror.
Object outside focus gives a real and inverted image.
Object between focus and pole gives a virtual, erect, and enlarged image.

notes.heading: Image Formation by Convex Mirror
notes.text: A convex mirror is a diverging mirror. It always forms a virtual, erect, and diminished image behind the mirror for a real object.
points: In a convex mirror, focus F and centre of curvature C lie behind the mirror.
Ray rule 1: A ray parallel to the principal axis appears to come from focus F after reflection.
Ray rule 2: A ray directed towards focus becomes parallel to the principal axis after reflection.
Ray rule 3: A ray directed towards centre of curvature returns along the same path after reflection.
Object at infinity: image forms at F behind the mirror, virtual, erect, and highly diminished.
Object at any finite distance: image forms between P and F behind the mirror, virtual, erect, and diminished.
Convex mirrors are used as rear-view mirrors because they form erect images and give a wide field of view.
The image in a convex mirror is smaller than the object, so objects appear farther than they actually are.
For a convex mirror, magnification m = -v/u is usually positive and less than 1 in magnitude.
Key point: A convex mirror always forms a virtual, erect, and diminished image.

notes.heading: Refraction of Light
notes.text: Refraction of light is the bending of light when it travels from one transparent medium to another transparent medium. Refraction occurs because the speed of light changes in different media.
points: A pencil partly dipped in water appears bent due to refraction.
Light travels fastest in vacuum and slower in air, water, and glass.
An optically rarer medium is one in which light travels faster.
An optically denser medium is one in which light travels slower.
When light travels from rarer medium to denser medium, it bends towards the normal.
When light travels from denser medium to rarer medium, it bends away from the normal.
Incident ray is the ray falling on the surface separating two media.
Refracted ray is the ray that bends and enters the second medium.
Angle of incidence i is the angle between incident ray and normal.
Angle of refraction r is the angle between refracted ray and normal.
First law of refraction: Incident ray, refracted ray, and normal at the point of incidence lie in the same plane.
Second law of refraction or Snell's law: sin i / sin r = constant.
The constant is the refractive index of the second medium with respect to the first: sin i / sin r = n21.
Important rule: Rarer to denser means towards normal; denser to rarer means away from normal.

notes.heading: Refractive Index
notes.text: Refractive index tells how much a medium bends light and how much the speed of light decreases in that medium.
points: Absolute refractive index: n = c/v.
c is speed of light in vacuum and v is speed of light in the medium.
Speed of light in vacuum: c = 3 × 10^8 m/s.
Since v < c in a material medium, n is greater than or equal to 1.
For vacuum, n = 1; for air, n is approximately 1.
A medium with higher refractive index is optically denser.
A medium with lower refractive index is optically rarer.
Order: n_glass > n_water > n_air.
If refractive index of glass is 1.5, light travels 1.5 times slower in glass than in vacuum.
Relative refractive index of medium 2 with respect to medium 1: n21 = v1/v2.
Relative refractive index can also be written as n21 = n2/n1.
Snell's law using refractive index: n1 sin i = n2 sin r.
Approximate refractive indices: vacuum 1.000, air 1.0003, water 1.33, glass 1.5, diamond 2.42.
Diamond bends light strongly because it has a high refractive index.

notes.heading: Refraction Through a Rectangular Glass Slab
notes.text: A rectangular glass slab is a transparent block of glass with parallel opposite faces. Light bends twice while passing through it: first while entering glass from air and then while coming out from glass to air.
points: At the first surface, light travels from air to glass and bends towards the normal.
At the second surface, light travels from glass to air and bends away from the normal.
The ray coming out of the slab is called the emergent ray.
For a rectangular glass slab, the emergent ray is parallel to the incident ray.
The emergent ray is shifted sideways from the original path.
Lateral displacement is the perpendicular distance between the original path of the incident ray and the emergent ray.
In a glass slab, opposite faces are parallel, so bending at the first surface is cancelled by bending at the second surface.
For a rectangular glass slab: i = e, where i is angle of incidence and e is angle of emergence.
Lateral displacement depends on thickness of slab, angle of incidence, and refractive index of glass.
Greater thickness gives greater lateral displacement.
Greater angle of incidence gives greater lateral displacement.
Higher refractive index gives greater bending and greater lateral displacement.
Printed letters appear shifted when seen through a glass slab due to refraction.

notes.heading: Apparent Depth and Real Depth
notes.text: When an object is inside water or glass, it appears to be at a different position from its actual position due to refraction. A coin at the bottom of water appears raised.
points: Real depth is the actual depth of an object inside a medium.
Apparent depth is the depth at which the object appears to be present.
Usually apparent depth is less than real depth.
A coin inside water appears raised because light travels from water to air and bends away from the normal.
The eye assumes light travels in a straight line, so rays appear to come from a point above the real position.
For small angles: n = Real depth / Apparent depth.
Apparent depth = Real depth / n.
Apparent shift = Real depth - Apparent depth.
Example: Real depth = 24 cm, refractive index of water n = 4/3.
Apparent depth = 24 ÷ (4/3) = 18 cm.
Apparent shift = 24 - 18 = 6 cm.
Daily examples: swimming pool appears shallow, coin appears raised in water, and fish appears above its real position.

notes.heading: Refraction at Spherical Surfaces
notes.text: A spherical refracting surface is a curved surface separating two transparent media. Light bends when it passes from one medium into another through such a curved surface.
points: The surface of a glass lens is an example of a spherical refracting surface.
Pole P is the centre point of the spherical refracting surface.
Centre of curvature C is the centre of the sphere of which the surface is a part.
Radius of curvature R is the distance between pole and centre of curvature: R = PC.
Principal axis is the line passing through pole and centre of curvature.
Formula for refraction at a spherical surface: n2/v - n1/u = (n2 - n1)/R.
n1 is refractive index of first medium, n2 is refractive index of second medium, u is object distance, v is image distance, and R is radius of curvature.
This formula is important because lens maker's formula is derived from it.
Sign convention: distances are measured from the pole, and direction of incident light is positive.
R is positive if centre of curvature lies in the direction of incident light.
R is negative if centre of curvature lies opposite to the direction of incident light.
For a plane refracting surface, R = infinity, so (n2 - n1)/R = 0.
For a spherical surface, R is finite and curvature affects image formation.

notes.heading: Thin Lenses
notes.text: A lens is a transparent optical object bounded by two surfaces, at least one of which is spherical. A thin lens has thickness very small compared to its radius of curvature. Lenses form images by refraction.
points: Main types of lenses are convex lens and concave lens.
A convex lens is thicker at the centre and thinner at the edges.
A convex lens is called a converging lens because parallel rays meet at focus after refraction.
Examples of convex lenses: magnifying glass, camera lens, and human eye lens.
A concave lens is thinner at the centre and thicker at the edges.
A concave lens is called a diverging lens because parallel rays spread out after refraction.
Examples of concave lenses: spectacles for myopia and door viewers.
Optical centre O is the central point of a lens.
A ray passing through the optical centre goes undeviated.
Principal axis is the straight line passing through optical centre and centres of curvature of the lens surfaces.
Principal focus F is the point where rays parallel to the principal axis meet or appear to meet after refraction.
Focal length f is the distance between optical centre and principal focus: f = OF.
A lens has two principal foci, F1 and F2, because light can pass through it from either side.
For a thin lens in the same medium on both sides, OF1 = OF2.
Convex lens has positive focal length.
Concave lens has negative focal length.
Ray rule: A ray through optical centre goes straight without deviation.
Ray rule: A ray parallel to principal axis passes through focus for a convex lens and appears to come from focus for a concave lens.
Ray rule: A ray passing through focus becomes parallel to principal axis after refraction.

notes.heading: Lens Formula and Magnification
notes.text: The lens formula is used to calculate object distance, image distance, or focal length for thin lenses. Magnification tells the size of the image compared to the object.
points: Lens formula: 1/v - 1/u = 1/f.
v is image distance, u is object distance, and f is focal length.
For lenses, the optical centre O is taken as origin.
Direction of incident light is taken as positive.
Distances measured opposite to incident light are negative.
Object distance u is usually negative.
Convex lens has positive focal length.
Concave lens has negative focal length.
Real image has positive image distance v because it is formed on the other side of the lens.
Virtual image has negative image distance v because it is formed on the same side as the object.
Magnification by lens: m = hi/ho = v/u.
If m is positive, image is virtual and erect.
If m is negative, image is real and inverted.
If |m| > 1, image is enlarged.
If |m| < 1, image is diminished.
If |m| = 1, image is same size as object.
Example: For a convex lens with f = +10 cm and u = -30 cm, 1/v - 1/u = 1/f gives v = 15 cm.
For this example, m = v/u = 15/(-30) = -1/2.
The image is real, inverted, and half the size of the object.
Mirror magnification is m = -v/u, while lens magnification is m = v/u.

notes.heading: Image Formation by Convex Lens
notes.text: A convex lens is a converging lens. It brings parallel rays together at the focus after refraction and forms different images depending on object position.
points: A convex lens can form real and inverted, virtual and erect, enlarged, diminished, or same-size images.
Important points are optical centre O, focus on object side F1, focus on image side F2, 2F1, and 2F2.
Ray rule 1: A ray parallel to principal axis passes through F2 after refraction.
Ray rule 2: A ray through optical centre O goes straight without deviation.
Ray rule 3: A ray passing through F1 becomes parallel to principal axis after refraction.
Object at infinity: image forms at F2, real, inverted, and highly diminished.
Object beyond 2F1: image forms between F2 and 2F2, real, inverted, and diminished.
Object at 2F1: image forms at 2F2, real, inverted, and same size; m = -1.
Object between F1 and 2F1: image forms beyond 2F2, real, inverted, and enlarged.
Object at F1: image forms at infinity, real, inverted, and highly enlarged.
Object between F1 and O: image forms on the same side as the object, virtual, erect, and enlarged.
A convex lens acts as a magnifying glass when the object is between focus and optical centre.
Object outside focus gives a real and inverted image.
Object between focus and lens gives a virtual, erect, and enlarged image.

notes.heading: Image Formation by Concave Lens
notes.text: A concave lens is a diverging lens. It spreads out parallel rays after refraction and always forms a virtual, erect, and diminished image for a real object.
points: A concave lens is thinner at the centre and thicker at the edges.
Concave lenses are used in spectacles to correct myopia.
A concave lens always forms an image on the same side as the object.
Important points are optical centre O, focus on object side F1, and focus on other side F2.
For a concave lens, the focus from which rays appear to diverge lies on the same side as the object.
Ray rule 1: A ray parallel to principal axis appears to come from F1 after refraction.
Ray rule 2: A ray through optical centre O goes straight without deviation.
Ray rule 3: A ray directed towards F2 becomes parallel to the principal axis after refraction.
Object at infinity: image forms at F1, virtual, erect, highly diminished, and on the same side as the object.
Object at any finite distance: image forms between O and F1, virtual, erect, diminished, and on the same side as the object.
For a concave lens, magnification m = v/u is positive and |m| < 1.
A concave lens forms a virtual image because refracted rays do not actually meet; they appear to meet when extended backward.

notes.heading: Power of a Lens
notes.text: Power of a lens tells how strongly a lens can converge or diverge light. A lens with shorter focal length has greater power.
points: Power of lens is defined as the reciprocal of focal length in metres.
Formula: P = 1/f.
SI unit of power is dioptre, represented by D.
A lens has power 1 D if its focal length is 1 m.
Convex lens has positive focal length and positive power.
Concave lens has negative focal length and negative power.
Example: If f = +0.5 m, P = 1/0.5 = +2 D.
If focal length is in centimetres, use P = 100/f(cm).
Example: A convex lens with f = +20 cm has power P = 100/20 = +5 D.
For thin lenses in contact, total power is P = P1 + P2 + P3 + ... .
Example: P1 = +3 D and P2 = -1 D give total power P = +2 D.
More power means more bending of light.
Positive power means convex lens; negative power means concave lens.

notes.heading: Combination of Thin Lenses in Contact
notes.text: When two or more thin lenses are placed very close together in contact, they behave like a single lens with an equivalent focal length and total power.
points: Lens combinations are used to increase or decrease total power, reduce image defects, improve sharpness, and make optical instruments.
For two thin lenses in contact: 1/F = 1/f1 + 1/f2.
F is equivalent focal length, f1 is focal length of first lens, and f2 is focal length of second lens.
For more than two lenses: 1/F = 1/f1 + 1/f2 + 1/f3 + ... .
Power of combination: P = P1 + P2 + P3 + ... .
Example: f1 = 20 cm and f2 = 30 cm. Then 1/F = 1/20 + 1/30 = 5/60, so F = 12 cm.
Example: f1 = +20 cm and f2 = -40 cm. Then 1/F = 1/20 - 1/40 = 1/40, so F = +40 cm.
A positive equivalent focal length means the combination behaves like a convex lens.
Example using power: P1 = +5 D and P2 = -2 D give total power P = +3 D.
Convex lens has positive focal length and power.
Concave lens has negative focal length and power.
The main advantage of lens combinations is that defects of one lens can be reduced by another suitable lens.
Power is easier to add than focal length.

notes.heading: Refraction Through a Prism
notes.text: A prism is a transparent optical object bounded by three rectangular surfaces and two triangular surfaces. A triangular glass prism bends light due to refraction.
points: Light bends twice while passing through a prism: once from air to glass and once from glass to air.
The bending of the emergent ray from the original direction is called deviation.
Angle of prism A is the angle between the two refracting surfaces of the prism.
Incident ray is the ray falling on the first surface of the prism.
Emergent ray is the ray coming out from the second surface.
Angle of incidence i is the angle between incident ray and normal at the first surface.
Angle of emergence e is the angle between emergent ray and normal at the second surface.
Angle of deviation δ is the angle between the original direction of incident ray and the emergent ray.
If r1 and r2 are angles of refraction inside the prism, then A = r1 + r2.
Deviation formula: δ = i + e - A.
Minimum deviation δm is the least value of angle of deviation for a prism.
At minimum deviation: i = e and r1 = r2 = A/2.
Refractive index of prism material at minimum deviation: n = sin((A + δm)/2) / sin(A/2).
For a small-angle prism: δ = (n - 1)A.
A prism can split white light into seven colours; this is dispersion.

notes.heading: Dispersion of Light by Prism
notes.text: Dispersion of light is the splitting of white light into seven colours when it passes through a prism. The seven colours form a spectrum.
points: White light is made up of different colours.
Each colour has a different wavelength.
Different colours travel with different speeds in glass and bend by different amounts.
The order of colours is VIBGYOR: Violet, Indigo, Blue, Green, Yellow, Orange, Red.
Violet light deviates the most.
Red light deviates the least.
Violet has the shortest wavelength and highest refractive index in glass.
Red has the longest wavelength and lowest refractive index in glass.
Wavelength order: λR > λO > λY > λG > λB > λI > λV.
Refractive index order in glass: nV > nI > nB > nG > nY > nO > nR.
Angular dispersion is the angle between emergent violet ray and emergent red ray.
Formula: θ = δV - δR.
Since violet deviates more, δV > δR.
A rainbow is a natural spectrum formed due to refraction, dispersion, internal reflection, and again refraction in water droplets.
Deviation is bending of light from original path.
Dispersion is splitting of white light into colours.

notes.heading: Total Internal Reflection
notes.text: Total internal reflection is the phenomenon in which light is completely reflected back into the denser medium when it tries to travel from a denser medium to a rarer medium.
points: Examples of denser to rarer travel are glass to air and water to air.
Total internal reflection occurs only when light travels from denser medium to rarer medium.
The angle of incidence in the denser medium must be greater than the critical angle.
Condition: i > C.
Critical angle C is the angle of incidence in the denser medium for which the angle of refraction in the rarer medium is 90°.
At critical angle: i = C and r = 90°.
Using Snell's law at critical angle: n1 sin C = n2 sin 90°.
Critical angle formula: sin C = n2/n1, where n1 is denser medium and n2 is rarer medium.
For denser medium to air: sin C = 1/n.
If i < C, light refracts into the rarer medium.
If i = C, refracted ray travels along the boundary.
If i > C, light is totally internally reflected.
Diamond sparkles due to repeated total internal reflection because its critical angle is small.
Optical fibres work on total internal reflection.
Mirage is due to total internal reflection in layers of hot air near the ground.
Air bubbles in water appear bright due to total internal reflection.
Total internal reflection gives almost complete reflection and requires critical angle.

notes.heading: Optical Fibre
notes.text: An optical fibre is a thin, flexible, transparent fibre made of glass or plastic. It transmits light signals by repeated total internal reflection.
points: Optical fibre works on the principle of total internal reflection.
The central part of an optical fibre is called the core.
Light travels through the core.
The core has higher refractive index.
The outer covering around the core is called cladding.
Cladding has lower refractive index than core.
Condition: n_core > n_cladding.
In optical fibre, core acts as denser medium and cladding acts as rarer medium.
Light entering the fibre strikes the core-cladding boundary at angle greater than critical angle.
The light is reflected back into the core and continues by repeated total internal reflection.
Condition for working: i > C and n_core > n_cladding.
Advantages: fast communication, low loss, large bandwidth, light weight, flexibility, and no electromagnetic interference.
Uses: internet communication, telephone communication, cable TV, medical endoscopy, laser surgery, defence communication, and computer networking.
In endoscopy, optical fibres carry light into the body and bring the image back.
Optical fibre can guide light even through curved paths.

notes.heading: Lens Maker's Formula
notes.text: Lens maker's formula relates the focal length of a lens to the refractive index of its material and the radii of curvature of its two surfaces. It helps in designing lenses of required focal length.
points: Focal length of a lens depends on refractive index, curvature of the first surface, curvature of the second surface, and surrounding medium.
For a thin lens in air: 1/f = (n - 1)(1/R1 - 1/R2).
f is focal length, n is refractive index of lens material with respect to air, R1 is radius of curvature of first surface, and R2 is radius of curvature of second surface.
The first surface is the surface where light enters first.
The second surface is the surface from which light comes out.
For a biconvex lens with light from left to right: R1 is positive and R2 is negative.
For a biconcave lens with light from left to right: R1 is negative and R2 is positive.
General formula when lens is in another medium: 1/f = (n2/n1 - 1)(1/R1 - 1/R2).
n2 is refractive index of lens material and n1 is refractive index of surrounding medium.
Higher refractive index makes focal length shorter and lens more powerful.
More curved surfaces have smaller radius of curvature and bend light more strongly.
For a plane surface, R = infinity and 1/R = 0.
Example: For a biconvex lens with n = 1.5, R1 = +20 cm, R2 = -20 cm, 1/f = (1.5 - 1)(1/20 - 1/(-20)) = 1/20, so f = 20 cm.
Power form: P = (n - 1)(1/R1 - 1/R2), using metre units for dioptre.

notes.heading: Human Eye as an Optical Instrument
notes.text: The human eye is a natural optical instrument that works like a camera and forms images on the retina using a convex eye lens.
points: Cornea is the transparent front part of the eye where most refraction occurs.
Iris is the coloured part of the eye and controls pupil size.
Pupil is the small opening in the iris and controls the amount of light entering the eye.
In bright light, pupil becomes small; in dim light, pupil becomes large.
Eye lens is a convex lens that focuses light on the retina.
Retina is the light-sensitive screen at the back of the eye.
Image formed on the retina is real, inverted, and diminished.
The brain interprets the inverted image as erect.
Ciliary muscles hold the eye lens and change its shape.
Optic nerve carries signals from the retina to the brain.
Accommodation is the ability of the eye lens to change focal length to focus near and distant objects.
For distant objects, ciliary muscles relax, eye lens becomes thin, and focal length increases.
For nearby objects, ciliary muscles contract, eye lens becomes thick, and focal length decreases.
Least distance of distinct vision is the minimum distance at which an object can be seen clearly without strain.
For a normal human eye, least distance of distinct vision D = 25 cm.
Near point of a normal eye is 25 cm.
Far point is the farthest point up to which the eye can see clearly.
For a normal eye, far point is infinity.
Power of accommodation is the maximum change in power of the eye lens.
For a normal eye, power of accommodation is about 4 D.
Common defects of vision are myopia, hypermetropia, presbyopia, and astigmatism.

notes.heading: Defects of Vision and Their Correction
notes.text: A normal eye forms a clear image on the retina. Defects of vision occur when the eye cannot focus the image properly on the retina.
points: Main defects of vision are myopia, hypermetropia, presbyopia, and astigmatism.
Myopia is short-sightedness; a person can see nearby objects clearly but not distant objects.
In myopia, image forms in front of the retina.
Causes of myopia: eyeball becomes too long or eye lens becomes too powerful.
Myopia is corrected by a concave lens.
A concave lens diverges incoming rays before they enter the eye so that the eye lens focuses them on the retina.
Hypermetropia is long-sightedness; a person can see distant objects clearly but not nearby objects.
In hypermetropia, image forms behind the retina.
Causes of hypermetropia: eyeball becomes too short or eye lens has less converging power.
Hypermetropia is corrected by a convex lens.
A convex lens converges rays before they enter the eye so that image forms on retina.
Presbyopia is an age-related defect due to loss of power of accommodation.
Causes of presbyopia: ciliary muscles become weak, eye lens becomes less flexible, and lens cannot change focal length properly.
Presbyopia is corrected using suitable lenses, often bifocal lenses.
In bifocal lenses, the upper part is used for distant vision and lower part is used for near vision.
Astigmatism occurs when curvature of cornea or eye lens is not uniform.
Astigmatism causes blurred or distorted images and unequal clarity of lines in different directions.
Astigmatism is corrected by cylindrical lenses.
Memory trick: Myopia uses minus lens, which is concave.
Memory trick: Hypermetropia uses plus lens, which is convex.

notes.heading: Simple Microscope
notes.text: A simple microscope is an optical instrument used to see small objects clearly in magnified form. It is also called a magnifying glass.
points: A simple microscope uses a single convex lens of small focal length.
It works on the principle that a convex lens forms a virtual, erect, and magnified image when the object is placed between focus and optical centre.
Smaller focal length gives greater magnifying power.
In a simple microscope, the object is placed between F and O.
The image is formed on the same side as the object.
The image is virtual, erect, and enlarged.
Least distance of distinct vision for a normal eye is D = 25 cm.
Magnifying power is represented by M.
For final image at near point: M = 1 + D/f.
For final image at infinity: M = D/f.
Final image at near point gives more magnification but causes eye strain.
Final image at infinity gives relaxed vision.
Example: If f = 5 cm and D = 25 cm, then for near point M = 1 + 25/5 = 6.
For the same example at infinity, M = 25/5 = 5.
Uses of simple microscope: watch repair, jewellery work, biology, stamp collection, and laboratory observation.

notes.heading: Compound Microscope
notes.text: A compound microscope is an optical instrument used to see very tiny objects with high magnification. It uses two convex lenses: objective lens and eyepiece lens.
points: Objective lens is near the object.
Objective lens has small focal length and small aperture.
Eyepiece lens is near the eye and acts like a simple microscope.
The object is placed just beyond the focus of the objective lens.
The objective lens forms a real, inverted, and enlarged intermediate image.
The intermediate image acts as an object for the eyepiece.
The eyepiece forms the final virtual and magnified image.
The final image is virtual, inverted with respect to the original object, and highly enlarged.
Total magnifying power: M = mo × me.
For final image at near point: M = (L/fo)(1 + D/fe).
For final image at infinity: M = (L/fo)(D/fe).
L is tube length, fo is focal length of objective, fe is focal length of eyepiece, and D = 25 cm.
High magnification requires small objective focal length, small eyepiece focal length, and large tube length.
Example: If L = 20 cm, fo = 1 cm, fe = 5 cm, and D = 25 cm, then M = (20/1)(1 + 25/5) = 120 for final image at near point.
Simple microscope uses one convex lens and gives less magnification.
Compound microscope uses two convex lenses and gives higher magnification.

notes.heading: Astronomical Telescope
notes.text: An astronomical telescope is an optical instrument used to see distant heavenly bodies such as the Moon, stars, planets, and galaxies.
points: An astronomical telescope uses two convex lenses: objective lens and eyepiece lens.
Objective lens faces the distant object.
Objective lens has large focal length and large aperture.
Large aperture collects more light and makes the image brighter.
Eyepiece lens is near the eye and has small focal length.
A distant object sends nearly parallel rays.
The objective lens forms a real, inverted, and diminished image at its focus.
This image acts as an object for the eyepiece.
The final image is virtual, inverted, and magnified.
In normal adjustment, the final image is formed at infinity and gives relaxed vision.
For normal adjustment, length of telescope L = fo + fe.
Magnifying power in normal adjustment: M = fo/fe.
With sign convention: M = -fo/fe; the negative sign shows inversion.
For final image at near point: M = (fo/fe)(1 + fe/D).
For high magnifying power, objective focal length should be large and eyepiece focal length should be small.
Example: If fo = 100 cm and fe = 5 cm, then M = 100/5 = 20 and L = 100 + 5 = 105 cm.
A compound microscope is used for small nearby objects, while an astronomical telescope is used for distant objects.
Magnification of a telescope depends mainly on fo/fe.

notes.heading: Terrestrial Telescope
notes.text: A terrestrial telescope is an optical instrument used to see distant objects on Earth clearly. It gives an erect image, unlike an astronomical telescope.
points: It is used to see distant buildings, ships, mountains, birds, and land objects.
An astronomical telescope gives an inverted image, which is not suitable for terrestrial viewing.
A terrestrial telescope gives a virtual, erect, and magnified image.
It generally uses three convex lenses: objective lens, erecting lens, and eyepiece lens.
Objective lens has large focal length and large aperture.
Eyepiece lens has small focal length and acts as a magnifier.
Erecting lens is placed between objective and eyepiece.
The erecting lens makes the image upright.
Working: objective forms a real, inverted, diminished image; erecting lens makes it erect; eyepiece magnifies it.
Magnifying power is similar to astronomical telescope: M = fo/fe.
For high magnification, fo should be large and fe should be small.
Approximate length in normal adjustment: L = fo + 4f + fe, where f is focal length of erecting lens.
Because of the extra erecting lens, a terrestrial telescope is longer.
Some light is lost due to extra lenses.
Uses: surveying, navigation, defence observation, bird watching, and viewing ships from shore.

notes.heading: Reflecting Telescope
notes.text: A reflecting telescope is an optical instrument that uses a large concave mirror as the objective. It is mainly used in astronomy to observe distant heavenly bodies.
points: It is called a reflecting telescope because it works by reflection of light.
The objective is a large concave mirror instead of a lens.
Main parts are concave mirror, secondary mirror, and eyepiece.
The large concave mirror collects light from distant objects and forms a real image near its focus.
A secondary mirror reflects the image towards the eyepiece.
The eyepiece magnifies the image.
The final image is virtual, magnified, and inverted.
A concave mirror can collect a large amount of light, useful for faint stars and galaxies.
Advantages: no chromatic aberration, large aperture possible, easier support, cheaper for large size, and better for astronomy.
Mirrors reflect all colours equally, so they do not suffer from chromatic aberration.
Large mirrors can be made and supported more easily than large lenses.
Reflecting telescope uses a concave mirror as objective, while refracting telescope uses a convex lens as objective.
Most large modern astronomical telescopes are reflecting telescopes because they can collect more light.

notes.heading: Resolving Power of Optical Instruments
notes.text: Resolving power is the ability of an optical instrument to see two very close objects as separate objects. Higher resolving power means better clarity and more detail.
points: Resolution is the ability to distinguish two close objects separately.
Limit of resolution is the minimum separation between two objects so that they are just seen separately.
Smaller limit of resolution means better resolving power.
For a telescope, minimum angular separation: θ = 1.22λ/D.
λ is wavelength of light and D is diameter of the objective.
Resolving power of telescope = 1/θ = D/(1.22λ).
Resolving power of telescope increases when objective diameter D increases.
Resolving power of telescope increases when wavelength λ decreases.
For a microscope, limit of resolution: d = λ/(2n sin θ).
n is refractive index of medium and θ is half-angle of cone of light entering objective.
Numerical aperture = n sin θ.
Microscope limit of resolution: d = λ/(2 × Numerical Aperture).
Resolving power of microscope = 1/d = 2n sin θ/λ.
Resolving power of microscope increases by using shorter wavelength light.
Resolving power of microscope increases by increasing refractive index, such as using oil immersion.
Resolving power of microscope increases by increasing aperture angle θ.
Telescope resolving power mainly increases with objective diameter.
Microscope resolving power mainly increases with numerical aperture.

notes.heading: Important Formula Summary
notes.text: The chapter contains important formulas for mirrors, refraction, lenses, prisms, total internal reflection, microscopes, telescopes, and resolving power.
points: Mirror formula: 1/f = 1/v + 1/u.
Mirror magnification: m = hi/ho = -v/u.
Radius and focal length of spherical mirror: R = 2f or f = R/2.
Snell's law: n1 sin i = n2 sin r.
Relative refractive index: sin i/sin r = n21.
Absolute refractive index: n = c/v.
Relative refractive index: n21 = n2/n1 = v1/v2.
Apparent depth formula: n = Real depth/Apparent depth.
Apparent depth = Real depth/n.
Apparent shift = Real depth - Apparent depth.
Refraction at spherical surface: n2/v - n1/u = (n2 - n1)/R.
Thin lens formula: 1/v - 1/u = 1/f.
Lens magnification: m = hi/ho = v/u.
Lens maker's formula in air: 1/f = (n - 1)(1/R1 - 1/R2).
Power of lens: P = 1/f, where f is in metres.
Unit of power: dioptre (D).
If f is in cm: P = 100/f(cm).
Power of lenses in contact: P = P1 + P2 + P3 + ... .
Equivalent focal length of lenses in contact: 1/F = 1/f1 + 1/f2 + ... .
Prism relation: A = r1 + r2.
Angle of deviation of prism: δ = i + e - A.
Refractive index of prism at minimum deviation: n = sin((A + δm)/2)/sin(A/2).
Small-angle prism: δ = (n - 1)A.
Critical angle: sin C = n2/n1.
For denser medium to air: sin C = 1/n.
Condition for total internal reflection: i > C and light must travel from denser to rarer medium.
Simple microscope near point: M = 1 + D/f.
Simple microscope infinity: M = D/f.
Compound microscope near point: M = (L/fo)(1 + D/fe).
Compound microscope infinity: M = (L/fo)(D/fe).
Astronomical telescope normal adjustment: M = fo/fe; with sign M = -fo/fe.
Length of astronomical telescope in normal adjustment: L = fo + fe.
Astronomical telescope near point: M = (fo/fe)(1 + fe/D).
Resolving power of telescope: θ = 1.22λ/D and resolving power = D/(1.22λ).
Resolving power of microscope: d = λ/(2n sin θ) and resolving power = 2n sin θ/λ.
For mirrors: concave mirror has negative focal length and convex mirror has positive focal length.
For lenses: convex lens has positive focal length and concave lens has negative focal length.

notes.heading: Important Conceptual Notes
notes.text: Important concepts in ray optics are based on reflection, refraction, dispersion, total internal reflection, lenses, microscopes, and telescopes.
points: Light bends during refraction because its speed changes when it passes from one medium to another.
A pencil partly dipped in water appears bent because light from the part inside water refracts while travelling from water to air.
A swimming pool appears shallower because light from the bottom bends away from the normal when it comes from water to air.
A convex mirror is used as a rear-view mirror because it gives an erect image and wide field of view.
A concave mirror is used as a shaving mirror because it forms a virtual, erect, and enlarged image when the face is between pole and focus.
Diamond sparkles because of repeated total internal reflection due to its high refractive index and small critical angle.
Total internal reflection cannot occur from air to water because it requires light to travel from denser to rarer medium.
Optical fibre is useful for communication because light travels through it by repeated total internal reflection with low loss and high data capacity.
White light splits into colours in a prism because different colours have different wavelengths and refractive indices in glass.
In a prism, violet deviates most and red deviates least.
A convex lens is a converging lens because it bends parallel rays towards the principal axis and focuses them.
A concave lens is a diverging lens because it spreads parallel rays away from the principal axis.
Convex lens has positive power because its focal length is positive.
Concave lens has negative power because its focal length is negative.
A simple microscope uses a convex lens because it forms a virtual, erect, and magnified image.
A compound microscope gives high magnification because the objective forms an enlarged real image and the eyepiece further magnifies it.
The objective of a microscope should have small focal length because magnifying power increases when fo decreases.
The objective of a telescope should have large focal length because M = fo/fe.
The objective of a telescope should have large aperture to collect more light and produce brighter images.
Reflecting telescopes are preferred over refracting telescopes because they do not suffer from chromatic aberration and can have large apertures.

notes.heading: Important Numerical Problems
notes.text: Numerical problems in ray optics mainly use mirror formula, lens formula, magnification, power, refractive index, apparent depth, critical angle, microscope magnification, and telescope magnification.
points: Mirror formula example: For a concave mirror with f = -15 cm and u = -30 cm, 1/f = 1/v + 1/u gives v = -30 cm. The image is real, inverted, and same size.
Mirror magnification example: If u = -20 cm and v = -40 cm, then m = -v/u = -(-40)/(-20) = -2. The image is real, inverted, and twice the size.
Lens formula example: For a convex lens with f = +20 cm and u = -30 cm, 1/v - 1/u = 1/f gives v = +60 cm. The image is real and inverted.
Lens magnification example: If u = -25 cm and v = +50 cm, then m = v/u = 50/(-25) = -2. The image is real, inverted, and twice the size.
Power of lens example: For a convex lens with f = 25 cm, P = 100/25 = +4 D.
Focal length from power example: If P = -2 D, then f = 1/P = -0.5 m = -50 cm. Negative focal length means concave lens.
Combination of lenses example: If P1 = +5 D and P2 = -2 D, then total power P = +3 D. The combination behaves like a convex lens.
Refractive index example: If speed of light in a medium is 2 × 10^8 m/s and c = 3 × 10^8 m/s, then n = c/v = 1.5.
Apparent depth example: For real depth 24 cm and refractive index of water n = 4/3, apparent depth = 24/(4/3) = 18 cm.
Critical angle example: For glass-air interface with n_glass = 1.5, sin C = 1/1.5 = 2/3, so C ≈ 41.8° or about 42°.
Simple microscope example: If f = 5 cm and D = 25 cm, magnifying power at near point M = 1 + D/f = 1 + 25/5 = 6.
Astronomical telescope example: If fo = 100 cm and fe = 5 cm, then M = fo/fe = 20 and length L = fo + fe = 105 cm.
Important numerical formulas: 1/f = 1/v + 1/u, 1/v - 1/u = 1/f, m = -v/u for mirrors, m = v/u for lenses, P = 100/f(cm), n = c/v, and M = fo/fe.
Common numerical mistake is wrong sign convention.
Remember: concave mirror has negative focal length, convex lens has positive focal length, and object distance is usually negative.
